name: Membrane-Assisted Phase-Change Heat Sink
summary: Data centers spend a staggering share of their energy budget just moving heat, and conventional two-phase heat sinks are limited by unstable boiling. This project introduced a new cooling architecture: a porous membrane positioned above the heater surface that separates liquid and vapor paths and stabilizes two-phase flow. The result is heat fluxes up to 2 kW/cm², heat transfer coefficients up to 1.8 MW/m²K, and a 10x reduction in thermal resistance over state-of-the-art heat sinks. As a postdoc, I led a team of five graduate students and a postdoctoral researcher advancing the technology from a lab proof-of-concept (TRL 3) to a system demonstrator (TRL 6), including scalable packaging processes and techno-economic analyses for data center deployment.
blurb: A cooling paradigm delivering up to 2 kW/cm² and a 10x reduction in thermal resistance; led from TRL 3 to TRL 6.

name: Physics of Critical Heat Flux
summary: Critical heat flux — the point where a boiling surface suddenly and catastrophically fails to remove heat — has been an unsolved problem in thermal science for a century. It sets the safety limits of nuclear reactors and the power ceiling of electronics cooling. My PhD thesis resolved the forces acting on a departing bubble to show that the evaporation momentum limit is 3–4x higher than classical theory assumed, reconciling the two competing schools of thought (hydrodynamic instability vs. surface-based mechanisms). By stabilizing the flow near the surface we achieved a 6x increase in critical heat flux and a 5x improvement in heat transfer coefficient over the state of the art, and developed force-balance correlations that explain the physics rather than just fit it.

papers.label: Int. J. Heat & Mass Transfer 2023 (open manuscript)

blurb: Showed the evaporation momentum limit is 3–4x higher than a century of theory assumed; 6x increase in critical heat flux.

name: Bursting of Underwater Oil Drops
summary: When an oil drop rises to the water-air interface, the thin film above it ruptures — and what happens next determines how oil spreads across the ocean surface after a spill and how aerosols form above it. We uncovered the mechanisms that decide whether the drop spreads into a film, fragments into daughter droplets, or stays intact, and mapped the outcomes onto the drop's physical properties. Published in Physical Review Letters.
blurb: The mechanisms that decide whether a drop rising to the surface spreads, fragments, or stays intact.

name: Drop Impact on Sublimating Surfaces
summary: A water drop hitting dry ice freezes from below even as it spreads. We showed that the thickness of this solidified layer — set by the impact velocity — is the primary control on the outcome: enough solidification suppresses rebound and delays fragmentation. The findings inform the design of anti-icing and self-cleaning surfaces, where whether a drop sticks, bounces, or shatters is exactly what matters. Published in Physical Review Fluids.
blurb: How freezing from below delays fragmentation and suppresses rebound of impacting drops.

name: Drop Coalescence Simulation
summary: An axisymmetric volume-of-fluid simulation of a water drop coalescing into a flat pool, built on the Basilisk CFD framework with adaptive mesh refinement. The solver resolves the spreading dynamics through coalescence and tracks the full energy budget — kinetic, surface, and viscous dissipation — as the drop merges. The code is open source.
blurb: Axisymmetric volume-of-fluid simulation of a drop merging into a pool, built on Basilisk.

name: Coalescence & Spreading of Oil Drops on Water
summary: What happens when a drop of oil gently lands on water? My MS research answered this with a systematic study of 11 oils — n-alkanes, polymers, and hydrocarbons spanning a wide range of viscosity and spreading coefficient. We showed that the coalescence outcome is set by the drop's Ohnesorge number and that early-time spreading is oscillatory before the film settles, deriving scaling laws that matched experiments with R² = 0.99. The physics matters wherever oil meets water: oil spill mitigation, pharmaceutical emulsions, and targeted drug delivery. Published in the Journal of Colloid & Interface Science.
blurb: Scaling laws that predict whether an oil drop merges or spreads on water, matching experiments with R² = 0.99.

name: Optimizing Guide Rods of an Automotive Head Restraint
summary: Rear-end collisions account for 28% of all automobile crashes, and the resulting whiplash injury is governed largely by how well the headrest — and the guide rods that anchor it — restrain the occupant's head. For my undergraduate thesis, I designed and optimized the guide rods of an automotive head restraint to reduce whiplash injury risk. I modeled three guide-rod geometries in SolidWorks (13.8 mm OD stainless-steel tube, benchmarked against Mazda and Toyota production designs) and evaluated each with static structural FEA in ANSYS Workbench at three headrest height settings, under the ~221 N load corresponding to a 5g head acceleration in a low-speed rear impact. The optimized geometry withstood the crash load with the lowest equivalent stress and deformation across height settings — up to 42% lower peak stress than the conventional baseline (47.3 MPa vs 82.0 MPa at the highest setting) — and was identified as the best candidate for a safer headrest.
blurb: FEA-optimized head-restraint guide rods, cutting peak crash-load stress by 42% to reduce whiplash risk.
